articles.title: Sonia Slams Govt's Silence on Khamenei Killing, Demands Parliament Debate
articles.summary: Sonia Gandhi, former Congress president, criticized the government's silence on the killing of Iran's Supreme Leader Khamenei and demanded a parliamentary debate on the issue when the Budget session resumes. She argued that the government's inaction reflects a breakdown in international order, which requires open discussion. The demand highlights political tensions over foreign policy and India's diplomatic positioning.
articles.raw_summary: The former Congress president also demanded that when Parliament reconvenes for the second part of the Budget session, the government's "disturbing silence" over the breakdown of international order must be debated openly and without evasion.

These rules aim to streamline tax dispute handling and ensure uniformity in tax administration. The changes are significant for taxpayers and tax professionals as they formalize appeal and dispute resolution processes under Sections 375, 376, and 379 of the Income Tax Act.

This move is part of India's commitment to energy efficiency and climate goals.

articles.title: Appeal Rejected as Not Maintainable Because Filed Before Incorrect ITAT Bench
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) rejected an appeal because it was filed before the wrong bench. The ruling clarifies that appeals must be filed before the correct bench with territorial jurisdiction. The tribunal allowed the appellant to refile the appeal with a delay condonation petition. This decision impacts taxpayers and legal practitioners dealing with tax disputes.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that an appeal filed before a Bench lacking territorial jurisdiction is not maintainable and cannot be transferred. Liberty was granted to approach the correct Bench with a delay condonation petition. The post Appeal Rejected as Not Maintainable Because Filed Before Incorrect ITAT Bench appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: GST on Recipient for “Non-Existent” Supplier: Legal Position & Case Law
articles.summary: Indian courts have questioned the practice of denying input tax credit (ITC) to buyers while taxing their outward supplies, calling suppliers 'non-existent.' The judiciary is protecting genuine buyers and rejecting inconsistent fact-finding that leads to cascading taxation. This ruling has significant implications for GST compliance and tax administration in India.
articles.raw_summary: Courts have questioned the practice of denying ITC by calling suppliers “non-existent” while simultaneously taxing outward supplies. The emerging judicial view protects bona fide buyers and rejects inconsistent fact-finding that causes cascading taxation. The post GST on Recipient for “Non-Existent” Supplier: Legal Position   Case Law appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Enforcement, yes — but with accountability: Understanding arrest provisions in GST law
articles.summary: The article explains the arrest provisions under India's GST law, emphasizing that while tax evasion cases justify such measures, arrests should be made with accountability. Former CBIC chairman Najib Shah highlights that legal grounds alone do not mandate arrests, as per the Board's circular. This discussion is relevant for understanding how fiscal laws are enforced in India.
articles.raw_summary: Given the large-scale detection of tax evasion cases, which have a multiplier effect on the economy and may even fund anti-national activities, arrest provisions are necessary in fiscal laws. However, the Board’s circular makes it clear that the mere existence of legal grounds for arrest does not automatically mandate an arrest, writes former CBIC chairman Najib Shah.

articles.title: Non-Parties Liable For Contempt If They Knowingly Aid Disobedience Of Orders: Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that individuals or entities not involved in original court proceedings can still be held liable for contempt if they knowingly aid or ignore disobedience of court orders. The judgment emphasizes that awareness of a judicial order makes deliberate inaction or assistance in non-compliance punishable. This ruling strengthens judicial authority and accountability in India's legal system.
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court has clarified that even persons who were not parties to the original proceedings can be held liable for contempt if they knowingly aid or facilitate the disobedience of a court order. The Court emphasised that once a person or authority becomes aware of a judicial order, deliberate inaction or assistance in non-compliance can amount to contempt of court. A bench of Justice Ahsanuddin Amanullah and Justice R. Mahadevan made the observations while dealing with contempt petitions

articles.title: CBIC introduces deferred customs duty payment facility for manufacturers-importers
articles.summary: The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs has introduced a new facility for manufacturer-importers to defer customs duty payments. This move aims to boost cash flow and support domestic manufacturing. Eligible firms can now pay duties monthly instead of at clearance.
articles.raw_summary: The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs is launching a new facility for manufacturer importers. Approved companies can now defer customs duty payments. This move aims to boost cash flow and support domestic manufacturing. The scheme will be operational from April 1, 2026, to March 31, 2028. articles.title: When Does A Broken Promise To Marry Turn Into A Crime?
articles.summary: For decades, criminal courts in India have grappled with a singular, messy question: When does a broken promise to marry turn into a crime? Under the Indian Penal Code this was usually dealt under Section 375, specifically relying on the "misconception of fact" clause. The courts were often forced to fit complex relationship dynamics into the rigid framework of rape laws. The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita attempts to fix this by carving out a specific niche. Section 69 introduces a distinct offense i.

Section 69 introduces a distinct offense i.

Analysts warn that prices may face resistance near $77, but inflation and geopolitical risks could push them higher. This could affect India's fuel imports and economic planning.
articles.raw_summary: Oil prices climbed above $70 as escalating US-Iran tensions raised fears of supply disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz. Analysts warn that while momentum remains strong, resistance near $77 could trigger consolidation. Markets are weighing inflation risks and the possibility of further geopolitical escalation driving crude higher.

articles.title: Gulf War to Operation Sindoor: What India's stock market reactions to past geopolitical risks reveal amid US-Iran conflict
articles.summary: Amidst the backdrop of international political unrest, Indian markets showcase an impressive ability to weather the storm. With strong economic buffers and substantial domestic liquidity, India stands firm against external shocks.

articles.title: 'Could be largest oil supply disruption in history', S&P Global Energy warns on US-Iran war
articles.summary: S&P Global Energy warns that a US-Iran war could cause the largest oil supply disruption in history, potentially leading to a 7 million barrels per day loss. This could drive oil prices to $100 per barrel or higher, affecting countries like India with limited oil inventories. The disruption could force supply rationing while demand falls, impacting global and Indian energy markets.
articles.raw_summary: S P Global Energy says that markets with little or no inventories will face the brunt of the negative supply impact and in case there is a 7 million barrels per day loss in supply for many months, prices will indeed rise to $100 per barrel or even higher as supply is rationed while demand falls.

articles.title: 2 more panels for Bengal to fast-track CAA applications
articles.summary: The Indian Home Ministry has set up two additional empowered committees in West Bengal to speed up citizenship applications under the Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA). These panels will process applications for persecuted minorities from Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh who arrived in India before December 31, 2014. This move aims to streamline the citizenship process for eligible applicants in the state.

These panels will finalize citizenship grants for persecuted minorities from Pakistan, Afghanistan, and Bangladesh who arrived before December 31, 2014.

articles.title: Boardroom gaps widen across state-run corporate giants

This pervasive issue demands immediate attention to prevent further deterioration of governance standards.

articles.title: Double Jeopardy Does Not Bar PMLA Prosecution: SC Clarifies ‘Same Offence’ Test
articles.summary: The Supreme Court has clarified that parallel prosecutions under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA) do not violate the constitutional protection against double jeopardy, as money laundering is considered a distinct offence from the predicate crime. This ruling ensures that individuals can be prosecuted under both the PMLA and the underlying crime without violating Article 20(2) of the Constitution, provided there is no identity of the same offence.
articles.raw_summary: Courts have held that money laundering is a distinct offence from the predicate crime. Parallel prosecutions under PMLA do not violate Article 20(2) unless there is identity of the same offence. The post Double Jeopardy Does Not Bar PMLA Prosecution: SC Clarifies ‘Same Offence’ Test appeared first on TaxGuru .
